conjectures.title: CSP presence-only is insufficient
conjectures.hypothesis: A CSP of 'default-src *' or 'unsafe-inline unsafe-eval' provides zero XSS protection yet passes current check. Parsing policy directives will catch >80% of weak CSPs against haxgoat.
evidence: haxgoat /weak-csp: default-src * unsafe-inline unsafe-eval now correctly FAILs
7 CSP tests pass: missing/strong/weak/unsafe-inline-only/wildcard/no-default/granular

conjectures.title: HSTS max-age=0 is a security regression
conjectures.hypothesis: max-age=0 disables HSTS per RFC 6797. Parsing the numeric value and failing on <31536000 will catch all HSTS downgrade misconfigurations.
evidence: haxgoat /hsts-zero: max-age=0 now correctly FAILs with RFC 6797 citation
7 HSTS tests pass: missing/maxage-zero/short/no-includesubdomains/full/preload/parser

conjectures.title: CORS origin reflection is more dangerous than wildcard
conjectures.hypothesis: Sending Origin: evil.example.com and checking if reflected in ACAO will detect the most critical CORS misconfiguration that wildcard-only check misses.
conjectures.review_gate: Is CORS reflection detection within hax scope (predicate, not scanner)?

conjectures.title: Idempotency requires state comparison not status codes
conjectures.hypothesis: RFC 9110 defines idempotency as same server-side effect, not same response. A GET-after-PUT comparison will have fewer false positives than status code comparison.
conjectures.review_gate: Does state comparison require protocol changes or just predicate logic?

conjectures.title: Relevance matrix eliminates nonsense mutation-predicate pairs
conjectures.hypothesis: Routing mutations to semantically relevant predicate groups via a static matrix will reduce test count by >60% while maintaining full coverage.
conjectures.review_gate: Is relevance matrix reduction measurable against current test suite?

conjectures.title: Multi-request predicate signature enables 11 stub predicates
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding MultiPredicate func(client, target) Result to NamedPred unblocks idempotency, safety, csrf, 304, replay, toctou, workflow-skip, retries, cors-reflection, jsonp, rate-limit without changing existing single-response predicates.
conjectures.review_gate: Does MultiPredicate signature change break existing predicate contract?

conjectures.title: SameSite=None without Secure is a browser-rejected cookie
conjectures.hypothesis: Parsing SameSite attribute value and checking for Secure flag co-presence will catch the silent cookie rejection that current substring check misses.
evidence: haxgoat /samesite-none-insecure: SameSite=None without Secure now correctly FAILs
11 SameSite tests pass: no-cookies/missing-attr/none-no-secure/none-with-secure/strict/lax/mixed

conjectures.title: Mutation Apply violates immutability of input request
conjectures.hypothesis: Deep-copying Headers map in each mutator will prevent Apply from modifying the original request, provable by test: original.Headers unchanged after Apply.
evidence: 4 regression tests: header-forge, header-corrupt, origin-cross-site, repeat-concurrent all prove original unchanged
copyHeaders() deep-copies map before mutation; all 8 mutators unexported (public API: Get/Apply only)

conjectures.title: Request type in generator creates semantic confusion and cycle risk
conjectures.hypothesis: Extracting Request to internal/request/ will eliminate all generator imports that exist solely for the type (mutation, executor), reduce import edges by 2, and prevent future cycles.
evidence: grep -rn generator.Request internal/ returns 0 matches. mutation and executor now import request, not generator.
Import edges reduced: mutation no longer depends on generator. Generator depends on request (leaf). No cycles possible.

conjectures.title: Predicate split reduces blast radius without API changes
conjectures.hypothesis: Splitting predicate.go into 7 files (types, runner, headers, methods, crossorigin, cache, state) within the same package will pass all existing tests with zero import or API changes.
evidence: 8 files created, predicate.go deleted. go build + go test pass. Zero API/import changes.
NamedPred.Type field added: TypeUniversal=1, TypeRelational=2, TypeSequential=3. Group name constants exported.

conjectures.title: cmdList hardcoded data diverges from predicate package
conjectures.hypothesis: Replacing cmdList string literals with predicate.AllGroups() iteration will show cors-reflection and referrer-policy that are currently hidden from hax list output.
evidence: hax list groups now shows cors-reflection and referrer-policy — were hidden in hardcoded version
23 predicates listed dynamically from predicate.AllGroups() — was 19 hardcoded

conjectures.title: Ollama image gen requires MLX on Apple Silicon for usable performance
conjectures.hypothesis: Without MLX dynamic library, x/z-image-turbo (12GB) and x/flux2-klein:4b (5.7GB) fall back to CPU and produce no output in 90s. With MLX, the reference project (qwen3-steering) generated images in ~65s.
evidence: x/z-image-turbo ran 90s with MLX WARN, produced 0 bytes of image output, only ANSI spinner codes
qwen3-steering reference: same model with MLX produced 01-activation-steering_x_flux2-klein_4b_s42.png (458KB) in 65.2s

conjectures.title: TLA+: Shrink terminates and preserves failure monotonically
conjectures.hypothesis: The oracle Shrink algorithm can be modeled as a TLA+ state machine over a 5-dimensional lattice (headers×auth×origin×method×repeat). The spec should prove: (1) every accepted step strictly decreases a well-founded complexity measure, (2) the failure property is an inductive invariant across all transitions, (3) MaxAttempts is a redundant bound (termination holds without it). If the greedy single-axis strategy can miss globally minimal counterexamples, TLA+ model checking on a bounded instance will produce a concrete witness.
conjectures.falsification: A TLC trace showing a shrink sequence that cycles, diverges, or accepts a non-failing simplification refutes the conjecture. A witness of a 2-dimensional step the greedy shrinker cannot find demonstrates the local-minimum limitation.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink.tla spec with 5 shrink dimensions modeled as state variables
Inductive invariant: current request always produces failure
Termination proof via well-founded ordering on (|headers|, auth_rank, origin_rank, method_rank, repeat)
TLC model check on bounded instance (4 headers, full auth/origin ladder, repeat<=5)
If local-minimum witness found, document as a known limitation with concrete example
evidence: TLC verified 2,500 distinct states: TypeOK, ComplexityDecreases, BoundedSteps, EventuallyDone all pass
MaxAttempts is provably redundant for termination — complexity is well-founded and strictly decreasing
Local-minimum limitation documented: greedy single-axis strategy cannot find minima requiring 2+ simultaneous dimension changes
Spec: formal/ShrinkTermination.tla, config: formal/ShrinkTermination.cfg
conjectures.review_gate: Does TLA+ shrink model fit hax scope (falsifier, not verifier)?

conjectures.title: Alloy: NamedPred tagged union well-formedness and runner dispatch soundness
conjectures.hypothesis: The NamedPred type system (Type field vs Fn/ReqFn/MultiFn population) can be modeled as an Alloy signature with a multiplicity constraint: exactly one function field is populated per type tag. Running the Alloy analyzer on the current predicate definitions will find the 8 type/function mismatches (BUG-1 from invariant review) as counterexamples to the well-formedness constraint. Additionally, modeling Run/RunWithRequest/RunMulti dispatch as Alloy predicates will reveal whether any predicate is unreachable (never dispatched by any runner).
conjectures.falsification: If Alloy finds zero counterexamples to the exclusivity constraint, BUG-1 was misidentified. If all predicates are reachable under current dispatch rules despite the type mismatches, the bug has no practical impact.
acceptance_criteria: Alloy model with NamedPred sig, Type enum, three optional function fields
Constraint: Type=Universal => Fn set, ReqFn+MultiFn unset (and similarly for other types)
Instance generation reproducing the 8 known mismatches as counterexamples
Dispatch model: Run/RunWithRequest/RunMulti as predicates over NamedPred
Reachability analysis: which predicates are dispatched by which runner
Document whether type-tag-based or structural dispatch is the intended semantics
conjectures.review_gate: Does Alloy NamedPred model inform implementation or is it academic exercise?

conjectures.title: Dafny: Mutation operators preserve value semantics and relevance matrix is a valid routing
conjectures.hypothesis: Each mutation operator can be specified in Dafny with a postcondition: the original request's Headers map is unmodified (frame condition). The ensures clause `old(req.Headers) == req.Headers` should be provable for all 8 operators after copyHeaders. Additionally, the relevance matrix can be modeled as a function from Mutation→set<Group> with the completeness property: every mutation in AllOperators appears in at least one mapping, and every group in GroupNames is reachable. Dafny's verifier should either prove these or produce a counterexample for originSameSiteMutator (which skips copyHeaders).
conjectures.falsification: If Dafny proves the frame condition for originSameSiteMutator without copyHeaders, the shallow-copy risk identified in the invariant review is not real (Go's value semantics protect it). If the relevance completeness proof fails, there is a dead mutation or unreachable group.
acceptance_criteria: Dafny model of Request type with Headers as map<string,string>
Each mutator as a method with ensures old(req.Headers) == req.Headers
Verification of all 8 operators (expect originSameSite to require annotation or fail)
Relevance matrix as a total function with completeness lemma
Document whether Go value semantics make the copyHeaders call formally redundant for non-map-writing mutators
conjectures.review_gate: Does Dafny mutation proof inform Go implementation or stay theoretical?

conjectures.title: Lean4: Predicate type algebra and property classification theorem
conjectures.hypothesis: The three predicate types (Universal, Relational, Sequential) form a subtyping lattice where Type 1 ⊂ Type 2 ⊂ Type 3 (every universal property can be checked relationally by ignoring the request, every relational property can be checked sequentially by sending one request). This can be formalized in Lean4 as an inductive type with coercion functions. A theorem should prove: the composition of a Type-k predicate with a mutation of relevance-arity n is well-typed iff k >= n's minimum required type. This gives a type-theoretic foundation for the relevance matrix.
conjectures.falsification: If a Type 1 predicate genuinely cannot be lifted to Type 2 (the response alone is insufficient context even with a trivial request), the subtyping claim is false. The CORS reflection predicate (Type 2) is a candidate: it REQUIRES the request to determine correctness.
acceptance_criteria: Lean4 inductive type for PredicateType with three constructors
Coercion functions: Universal→Relational→Sequential with proofs they preserve semantics
Formalization of the relevance matrix as a dependent type: TestCase (m : Mutation) (gs : List Group) (min_type : PredicateType)
Theorem: well-typedness of mutation×predicate composition
At least one example predicate from each type fully formalized with its RFC property statement
Discussion: is the subtyping lattice the right model, or should types be disjoint with explicit lifting?
conjectures.review_gate: Does Lean4 type algebra change how predicates are dispatched?

conjectures.title: TLA+: HTTP method safety and idempotency as state machine properties
conjectures.hypothesis: RFC 9110 §9.2.1 (safe methods) and §9.2.2 (idempotent methods) can be modeled as a TLA+ state machine where server state is an integer counter and HTTP methods are actions. Safety = GET/HEAD/OPTIONS do not change state. Idempotency = PUT/DELETE applied N times yield same state as once. The spec should model hax's checkIdempotencyMulti and checkSafetyMulti predicates and verify they correctly detect violations. This provides a reference oracle: if the TLA+ model says 'violation' and hax says 'pass', hax has a false negative.
conjectures.falsification: If the TLA+ model shows that status-code comparison (which hax avoids per C-004) is equivalent to state comparison for the bounded model, conjecture 4 (idempotency requires state comparison) is weakened.
acceptance_criteria: HTTPServer.tla with state variable, GET/PUT/POST/DELETE actions per RFC 9110
Safety invariant: Safe methods do not modify state
Idempotency invariant: Idempotent methods applied twice yield same state as once
Model of hax's checkIdempotencyMulti logic as a TLA+ operator
TLC counterexample showing a server that passes status-code check but fails state check (validates C-004)
Bounded model: state space <= 10 values, methods = {GET, PUT, POST, DELETE}
evidence: TLC verified 276,965 distinct states: SafeMethodsPreserveState and IdempotentPUTConverges both hold
StatusCodeIdempotency_SHOULD_FAIL violated as expected: PUT returns 201 (create) then 200 (update) — different codes, same state
This validates C-004: hax correctly uses state comparison (response body diff) rather than status code comparison
Spec: formal/HTTPMethodSemantics.tla, configs: formal/HTTPMethodSemantics.cfg and HTTPMethodSemantics_StatusCode.cfg
conjectures.review_gate: Does TLA+ method model produce actionable test oracle improvements?

conjectures.title: CORS preflight absence is a testable gap
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding a cors-preflight predicate (Type 3) that sends OPTIONS before a non-simple cross-origin request will detect servers that skip preflight validation. Testing against haxgoat /cors-reflect and /api/transfer will find at least 2 endpoints that serve CORS headers on OPTIONS without validating the method/headers against an allowlist.
conjectures.falsification: If haxgoat endpoints already reject unauthorized preflight methods and the predicate produces zero failures, the gap is theoretical rather than practical.
conjectures.review_gate: Is CORS preflight a Type 3 predicate or does it require new executor capability?

conjectures.title: Presence-only predicates produce false passes on semantically invalid headers
conjectures.hypothesis: The 4 presence-only predicates (corp, permissions-policy, etag, vary) will pass on responses with syntactically valid but semantically insecure values. Adding value validation will convert at least 2 current passes to warns or fails against haxgoat endpoints.
conjectures.falsification: If haxgoat never returns invalid values for these headers (only missing or correct), upgrading to semantic checks produces no new findings.
conjectures.review_gate: Do semantic header checks stay within Type 1 or require application context?

conjectures.title: Vary: Origin omission enables CORS-based cache poisoning
conjectures.hypothesis: CORS-enabled responses that omit Vary: Origin allow cache poisoning where a same-origin response is served from cache to a cross-origin request. A new vary-origin predicate will find at least 1 vulnerable endpoint in haxgoat.
conjectures.falsification: If haxgoat endpoints already include Vary: Origin on CORS responses, or if Go's net/http automatically adds it, the predicate produces no findings.
conjectures.review_gate: Is Vary:Origin a standalone predicate or part of existing cache group?

conjectures.title: Conditional request predicates close the largest cache group gap
conjectures.hypothesis: Implementing the 304 predicate as Type 3 (GET to obtain ETag, then conditional GET with If-None-Match) will pass against haxgoat /cached-ok and fail/skip against /cached. Together with If-Match for optimistic concurrency, raises cache group from 0 to 2 functional predicates.
conjectures.falsification: If haxgoat /cached-ok does not properly return 304 on conditional GET, the predicate is correct but the test server is buggy.
conjectures.review_gate: Does 304 predicate need new executor features (conditional request support)?

conjectures.title: Cookie prefix validation catches strongest cookie misconfigurations
conjectures.hypothesis: RFC 6265bis S4.1.2.5 cookie prefixes (__Secure-, __Host-) are the strongest cookie security mechanism. A cookie-prefix predicate checking these constraints will catch misconfigured cookies that bypass SameSite protections.
conjectures.falsification: If no production servers use cookie prefixes (adoption near-zero), the predicate is theoretically correct but practically irrelevant at L1.
conjectures.review_gate: Are cookie prefixes observable at L1 or do they require browser context?

conjectures.title: RFC citation density correlates with predicate correctness
conjectures.hypothesis: Predicates citing specific RFC sections (hsts, idempotency, safety) have zero confirmed false negatives. Predicates without citations have confirmed gaps. Adding RFC citations to all 25 predicates will surface at least 3 implementation gaps.
conjectures.falsification: If adding citations to all predicates produces zero new implementation changes, the correlation is coincidental.
conjectures.review_gate: Is RFC citation density a methodology improvement or busywork?

conjectures.title: TRACE method disabled is a low-cost high-signal security predicate
conjectures.hypothesis: RFC 9110 S9.3.8 TRACE is routinely disabled to prevent XST attacks. A trace-disabled predicate (Type 3) sending TRACE and checking for 405/501 will find servers that leave TRACE enabled, including haxgoat's default ServeMux.
conjectures.falsification: If Go's default ServeMux returns 405 for TRACE, the predicate passes trivially against haxgoat.
conjectures.review_gate: Is TRACE detection a property check or a scan (anti-goal: not a scanner)?

conjectures.title: Open redirect detection requires parameter injection not mutation
conjectures.hypothesis: A new open-redirect predicate (Type 2) appending ?url=https://evil.example.com and checking Location header reflection will detect open redirects without a new mutation operator.
conjectures.falsification: If the predicate cannot distinguish legitimate redirects from open redirects without knowing application-specific URL parameter names, it produces false positives on every redirect.
conjectures.review_gate: Does open redirect detection require application-specific knowledge (violates L1)?

conjectures.title: h2spec naming convention (RFC-section/test-number) is adoptable for hax
conjectures.hypothesis: Renaming predicates from short names to RFC-indexed names (rfc6797-6.1.1, fetch-cors-3.2) makes coverage gaps mechanically detectable: any RFC section without a corresponding test is a gap.
conjectures.falsification: If the rename breaks CLI UX (names too long) or relevance matrix string coupling, the organizational cost outweighs traceability benefit.
conjectures.review_gate: Does h2spec naming break CLI UX or improve traceability?

conjectures.title: CSP base-uri and form-action are highest-impact missing CSP directives
conjectures.hypothesis: A CSP with default-src 'self' but no base-uri allows <base> injection hijacking all relative URLs. Adding base-uri and form-action checks to the CSP predicate catches these bypass techniques.
conjectures.falsification: If base-uri and form-action abuse requires attacker-controlled HTML injection (already a CSP bypass), these checks are redundant with script-src.
conjectures.review_gate: Are base-uri/form-action checks L1 properties or application-level (L2)?

conjectures.title: pgregory.net/rapid as test-only PBT dependency closes structural test gaps
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding rapid as a test-only dependency and writing property tests for the 6 core invariants (mutation value semantics, shrink termination, generator bounds, NamedPred type consistency, relevance completeness, Judge arithmetic) will find at least 1 bug the existing example-based tests miss. Properties over AllOperators() are closed under extension — adding mutator #9 automatically tests it. Rapid's integrated shrinking will produce minimal counterexamples without hand-written shrinkers.
conjectures.falsification: If rapid property tests over 10k iterations per invariant find zero new bugs beyond what the existing 4 hand-written deep-copy tests and fixed-input Judge test already cover, the value is marginal and the dep is not worth it.
acceptance_criteria: go.mod gains pgregory.net/rapid as test-only dependency; go build ./... still produces zero-dep binary
internal/mutation/mutation_property_test.go: value semantics across ALL mutators via AllOperators()
internal/oracle/oracle_property_test.go: Judge arithmetic (Total == sum) with arbitrary []Result; Shrink termination with arbitrary CheckFunc
internal/generator/generator_property_test.go: output bounded by Config for arbitrary configs including edge cases (single-element axes)
internal/predicate/predicate_property_test.go: NamedPred type/field exclusivity across AllGroups()
internal/relevance/relevance_property_test.go: matrix completeness against AllOperators() and GroupNames()
All property tests pass with go test -count=1 -rapid.checks=10000
conjectures.review_gate: Does rapid as test-only dep violate zero-dep philosophy for the binary?

conjectures.title: Hegel framework integration as deep verification layer via build tags
conjectures.hypothesis: When hegel-go ships, wrapping hax generators as Hegel strategies and running under //go:build hegel will answer whether Hypothesis's byte-level shrinker finds smaller counterexamples than hax's domain-specific lattice descent. If Hypothesis consistently finds smaller minimal failing inputs than oracle.Shrink, hax should delegate shrinking to Hegel. If not, the lattice structure is pulling its weight.
conjectures.falsification: If hegel-go never ships or the CBOR/Unix-socket overhead makes test cycles >10x slower than rapid with no improvement in counterexample minimality, the integration cost exceeds the verification value.
acceptance_criteria: Blocked on: hegel-go stable release
//go:build hegel test files in internal/oracle/ wrapping oracle.Shrink as a Hegel property
Side-by-side shrink comparison: same failure set, rapid shrinker vs Hegel/Hypothesis shrinker vs hax lattice shrinker
CI job with Python/uv available runs hegel-tagged tests separately from stdlib tests
Document whether byte-level or lattice-level shrinking produces smaller counterexamples for HTTP request domain
conjectures.review_gate: Is Hegel integration research or engineering? Blocked on hegel-go.

conjectures.title: Audit needs diff/compare mode for epoch-based regression tracking
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding --baseline <file> to hax audit that loads a prior YAML result and outputs a delta (new passes, regressions, resolved failures) will eliminate manual diff of JSON output between epochs. Against petstore-rails Epoch 0→1, the diff should show 5 resolved (csp, corp, coep, coop, permissions-policy), 0 regressions, 1 persistent (hsts). Without this, epoch tracking requires external tooling or eyeball comparison of full JSON blobs.
conjectures.falsification: If teams use external diff tools (jq, vimdiff) effectively and never request built-in comparison, the feature adds complexity without adoption.
evidence: petstore-rails Epoch 0: 7 pass, 3 fail, 3 warn. Epoch 1: 12 pass, 1 fail, 0 warn. Required manual comparison of two full JSON outputs to determine what changed.
conjectures.review_gate: Is baseline comparison a CLI concern or should it be delegated to external tooling?

conjectures.title: Profile-aware predicate skipping prevents false positives on known-inapplicable checks
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding --profile <file> to hax audit that reads a YAML profile (e.g. 'layers: [], notes: HSTS skipped') and suppresses predicates inapplicable to the deployment context will eliminate the persistent HSTS false positive on localhost. The profile mechanism should support skip-with-reason, so CI output shows 'hsts: skipped (profile: no HTTPS)' instead of 'hsts: fail'. Against petstore-rails local profile, this converts 1 failure to 1 annotated skip.
conjectures.falsification: If profile-based skipping masks real misconfigurations (developer sets profile to skip everything), the mechanism creates worse outcomes than false positives. Needs a safeguard like --strict that ignores profiles.
evidence: petstore-rails hax/profiles/local.yaml already documents 'HSTS and Secure cookie axioms skipped (no HTTPS)' but hax ignores this and reports HSTS as failure on every run
conjectures.review_gate: Does profile-based skipping violate the property-testing philosophy (never suppress a check)?

conjectures.title: Remediation hints in audit output close the developer feedback loop
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding a 'remediation' field to each failing/warning result with a one-liner fix and RFC section reference will reduce time-to-fix by eliminating the research step. For csp: 'Add Content-Security-Policy header (CSP L3 §2, RFC 9110 §8.1)'. For hsts: 'Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains (RFC 6797 §6.1)'. Enabled via --remediate flag to keep default output lean. Against petstore-rails Epoch 0, all 3 failures and 3 warnings would include actionable fix text.
conjectures.falsification: If developers already know what CSP/HSTS/CORP are and find remediation text patronizing or noisy, the feature degrades UX for experienced users. The --remediate flag mitigates this but adds flag surface area.
evidence: petstore-rails Epoch 0 failures say 'Content-Security-Policy header missing' but not how to add it. Developer must know Rails middleware patterns independently. Standard tools like bundler-audit and brakeman include fix suggestions.
conjectures.review_gate: Are remediation hints in scope for L1 property tester or is this L2 advisory territory?

conjectures.title: Skip-reason taxonomy distinguishes 'not applicable' from 'not implemented'
conjectures.hypothesis: The 12 skipped predicates in audit output conflate two distinct reasons: (a) the predicate is implemented but the response doesn't trigger it (e.g. 'no cookies set' for samesite), and (b) the predicate is a stub awaiting multi-request support (e.g. 'requires stateful test'). Adding a skip_reason enum (not_applicable | not_implemented | requires_feature) and a --hide-skip flag will let users filter noise. Against petstore-rails, 4 of 12 skips are 'not_applicable' and 8 are 'not_implemented' — users should be able to distinguish these.
conjectures.falsification: If users treat all skips uniformly and never filter by reason, the taxonomy adds structural complexity with no UX benefit.
evidence: petstore-rails audit: 12/25 predicates skipped. 'no cookies set' (legitimate skip) is visually identical to 'requires multi-request test' (hax limitation). Nearly half the output is uninformative noise for single-request audit.
conjectures.review_gate: Does skip-reason taxonomy change the Result type contract or is it metadata-only?

conjectures.title: Exit code semantics need --fail-on threshold for CI gate tuning
conjectures.hypothesis: hax audit exits non-zero on any failure, but CI pipelines need granular control: --fail-on=fail (default, current behavior), --fail-on=warn (exit non-zero on warn or fail), --fail-on=none (always exit 0, for advisory-only runs). Against petstore-rails Epoch 1, --fail-on=fail exits 1 (HSTS), but a team that uses profiles to skip HSTS wants exit 0. Without this, teams either ignore hax exit codes entirely or maintain wrapper scripts.
conjectures.falsification: If every team either wants strict (fail on anything) or advisory (ignore exit code), the middle ground (fail-on-warn) has no users and the flag is overengineered.
evidence: petstore-rails Epoch 0: exit 1 (3 fails + 3 warns). Epoch 1: exit 1 (1 fail, HSTS on localhost). No way to express 'HSTS on localhost is expected, exit 0' without a wrapper script or profile skip.
conjectures.review_gate: Does exit code granularity belong in hax or in CI wrapper scripts?

conjectures.title: Audit --record flag enables machine-written epoch history
conjectures.hypothesis: Adding --record <path> to hax audit that writes results in the existing hax/history/ YAML format will close the loop between running hax and tracking posture over time. Currently the YAML history file (hax/history/0.0.1.yaml) was hand-written. --record should auto-populate app_version (from --app-version flag or git), git_sha, date, profile, per-endpoint results, and failure/warning summaries. Against petstore-rails, --record hax/history/0.0.2.yaml after Epoch 1 fixes should produce a valid YAML that diffs cleanly against 0.0.1.yaml.
conjectures.falsification: If teams prefer to store hax results in their own format (JSON in CI artifacts, database, etc.) and the YAML history format has no adoption, maintaining the --record flag and YAML schema is dead weight.
evidence: petstore-rails hax/history/0.0.1.yaml exists but was manually created. No hax command produces this format. The gap between 'run hax' and 'record results for tracking' requires custom scripting or manual transcription.
conjectures.review_gate: Is result recording a core CLI concern or should hax output JSON and let external tools handle persistence?
